course_info.title: Linear Algebra
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

learning_objectives: Represent systems of linear equations as augmented matrices
Classify systems as consistent or inconsistent, and as having unique, infinite, or no solutions
Interpret systems geometrically as intersections of lines, planes, and hyperplanes
Translate real-world problems into systems of linear equations
readings: Lay, Linear Algebra and Its Applications, 6th Ed., Ch. 1.1-1.2
Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, 6th Ed., Ch. 1.1-1.2

lecture_notes.introduction: Linear algebra is the branch of mathematics most directly useful in computing, engineering, data science, and the physical sciences. Machine learning algorithms operate on vectors and matrices. Computer graphics transforms objects with matrix multiplications. Search engines rank pages using eigenvectors. Signal processing decomposes signals using orthogonal bases. Cryptographic systems rely on linear algebra over finite fields. Quantum computing is entirely formulated in linear algebra. This course builds the mathematical foundation for all of these applications, starting from the most concrete objects: systems of linear equations. A system of linear equations asks a simple question: what values of the unknowns simultaneously satisfy all of the equations? When there are two equations in two unknowns, the answer is the intersection point of two lines. When there are three equations in three unknowns, it is the intersection point of three planes. Beyond three dimensions, geometric intuition fails, but the algebraic methods we develop work in any number of dimensions. The augmented matrix representation strips away variable names and captures only the essential numerical relationships, enabling systematic solution methods that a computer can execute for systems with thousands of equations.
key_concepts: Linear equation: an equation where each term is either a constant or a constant times a single variable raised to the first power; no products of variables, no powers, no transcendental functions
System of linear equations: multiple linear equations that must be satisfied simultaneously by the same values of the unknowns
Augmented matrix: the coefficient matrix augmented with the constant vector, [A | b], encoding the entire system without variable names
Geometric interpretation: each equation defines a hyperplane; the solution is the intersection of all hyperplanes
Consistent system: at least one solution exists (the hyperplanes intersect); inconsistent system: no solution exists (the hyperplanes do not all intersect)
Unique solution: the hyperplanes intersect at exactly one point; infinitely many solutions: the hyperplanes intersect along a line, plane, or higher-dimensional subspace
Free variables: variables that can take any value in a system with infinitely many solutions; their count equals n - rank(A)
Homogeneous systems: systems of the form Ax = 0, which always have at least the trivial solution x = 0
Application examples: balancing chemical equations, network flow analysis, electrical circuit analysis, and economic input-output models
lecture_notes.demonstration: Solve a 3x3 system by hand, interpreting each equation as a plane in 3D space. Use GeoGebra or matplotlib to visualize the three planes intersecting at a single point. Then modify one equation to make the system inconsistent (parallel plane) and another to create infinitely many solutions (three planes meeting along a line). Show how the augmented matrix reveals each case.
setup_requirements: GeoGebra 3D or Python with matplotlib for plane visualization

review.value: For the system 2x + 3y = 7, x - y = 1, the augmented matrix is [[2, 3, | 7], [1, -1, | 1]]. The vertical bar separates the coefficient matrix from the right-hand side. All solution methods operate on this matrix using row operations.

Unique solution: each variable is determined (the system has as many pivot positions as unknowns). Infinitely many solutions: at least one free variable exists (more unknowns than pivots). No solution: a row of the form [0, 0, ..., 0, | b] with b nonzero appears, representing the impossible equation 0 = b.

The system Ax = 0 always has the trivial solution x = 0. It has nontrivial solutions if and only if A has more columns than pivot positions (the system has free variables). This happens whenever there are more unknowns than equations, or when the equations are linearly dependent.

review.key: Why Linear Algebra Matters for Computing
review.value: NumPy's fundamental data structure is the ndarray (matrix). TensorFlow and PyTorch perform millions of matrix operations per second during neural network training. Understanding the mathematical operations underneath enables you to debug, optimize, and innovate.

learning_objectives: Apply the three elementary row operations to transform an augmented matrix
Perform Gaussian elimination to achieve row echelon form
Perform Gauss-Jordan elimination to achieve reduced row echelon form
Determine the solution set from the reduced matrix including parameterized solutions

lecture_notes.introduction: Gaussian elimination is the workhorse algorithm of linear algebra. It systematically transforms any augmented matrix into a form where the solution can be read off directly, using only three elementary row operations that do not change the solution set. The algorithm works by creating zeros below each pivot, proceeding column by column from left to right, until the matrix is in row echelon form. Back substitution then solves for each variable starting from the last equation. Gauss-Jordan elimination goes further, creating zeros both below and above each pivot, producing the reduced row echelon form (RREF) from which the solution can be read without any back substitution. RREF is unique for every matrix, making it a canonical form that reveals the structure of the solution set completely. Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting (choosing the largest entry as the pivot to minimize rounding errors) is the standard algorithm used by numerical software including NumPy, MATLAB, and LAPACK. Understanding the algorithm is essential not just for solving systems by hand but for understanding the computational cost, numerical stability, and limitations of the methods your software uses. The algorithm requires approximately (2/3)n^3 floating point operations for an n by n system, making it practical for systems with thousands of equations but motivating iterative methods for very large systems.
key_concepts: Elementary row operations: (1) swap two rows, (2) multiply a row by a nonzero scalar, (3) add a scalar multiple of one row to another row
Row equivalence: two matrices are row equivalent if one can be obtained from the other by elementary row operations; row equivalent augmented matrices have the same solution set
Row echelon form (REF): leading entry of each nonzero row is to the right of the leading entry of the row above; all entries below each leading entry are zero; all zero rows are at the bottom
Reduced row echelon form (RREF): row echelon form where each leading entry is 1 and is the only nonzero entry in its column; RREF is unique for every matrix
Pivot positions: the positions of the leading 1s in RREF; they determine which variables are basic (determined) and which are free (parameterized)
Forward elimination: creating zeros below each pivot to reach REF; back substitution then solves from bottom to top
Gauss-Jordan method: creating zeros above and below each pivot to reach RREF; the solution is read directly from the final matrix
Computational complexity: Gaussian elimination on an n x n matrix requires approximately (2/3)n^3 multiply-add operations; this cubic growth limits direct methods for very large n
Partial pivoting: at each step, swap the row with the largest absolute value in the pivot column to the pivot position; reduces numerical errors from dividing by small numbers
Parameterized solutions: when free variables exist, express basic variables in terms of free variables to describe the full solution set
lecture_notes.demonstration: Step through Gaussian elimination on a 4x4 augmented matrix, explaining each row operation and the strategy for choosing operations. Show the difference between REF (with back substitution needed) and RREF (solution readable directly). Demonstrate the same system in NumPy using numpy.linalg.solve() and compare the result. Then solve a system with infinitely many solutions to demonstrate parameterized solutions with free variables.

review.value: An upper triangular staircase form where each leading entry (pivot) is to the right of the pivot above it, and all entries below each pivot are zero. This form enables back substitution: solve the last equation first, substitute into the second-to-last, and work upward.

Every matrix has exactly one RREF. This means two different sequences of row operations on the same matrix always produce the same RREF. This uniqueness makes RREF a reliable canonical form for comparing matrices and determining solution structure.

Gaussian elimination on an n x n matrix requires approximately (2/3)n^3 operations. For n=1000, that is about 667 million operations (fast). For n=1,000,000, that is about 6.67 x 10^17 operations (impractical). This motivates iterative methods (Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, conjugate gradient) for very large systems.

Without pivoting, dividing by a very small number amplifies rounding errors. Partial pivoting (choosing the largest available pivot) is essential in practice. NumPy and MATLAB use partial pivoting automatically. This is why hand computation and machine computation may give slightly different intermediate steps but the same final answer.

learning_objectives: Perform matrix addition, scalar multiplication, and matrix multiplication
Interpret matrix-vector multiplication as a linear combination of columns
Verify the properties of matrix arithmetic including non-commutativity of multiplication
Apply matrix operations to represent systems of equations in the form Ax = b

lecture_notes.introduction: Matrices are not just rectangular arrays of numbers; they are operators that transform vectors and encode relationships between variables. Matrix multiplication is the central operation of linear algebra, and understanding it deeply from multiple perspectives is essential. The most common perspective is the row-times-column dot product: entry (i,j) of AB is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B. But the column perspective is more illuminating: Ax is a linear combination of the columns of A, with the entries of x as weights. This means the product Ax produces a vector in the column space of A, which is the set of all possible outputs of the transformation represented by A. When we write a system of equations as Ax = b, we are asking: can b be expressed as a linear combination of the columns of A? Understanding matrix multiplication as transformation composition is equally important: if A represents one transformation and B represents another, then AB represents applying B first, then A. This is why matrix multiplication is not commutative: applying transformation A then B gives a different result than applying B then A, just as rotating then translating differs from translating then rotating.
key_concepts: Matrix addition and scalar multiplication: component-wise operations that are commutative and associative
Matrix multiplication definition: (AB)_ij = sum over k of A_ik * B_kj; requires the number of columns of A to equal the number of rows of B
Column perspective of Ax: the product Ax is a linear combination of the columns of A, with coefficients from x; Ax = x_1 * col_1(A) + x_2 * col_2(A) + ... + x_n * col_n(A)
Row perspective of Ax: row i of the product is the dot product of row i of A with x, computing one output coordinate at a time
Matrix multiplication as composition: AB represents applying B first, then A; the dimensions chain: if A is m x n and B is n x p, then AB is m x p
Non-commutativity: AB is generally not equal to BA; in fact, BA may not even be defined if the dimensions do not match
Matrix transpose: (A^T)_ij = A_ji; rows become columns; (AB)^T = B^T A^T (note the reversal of order)
Identity matrix: I_n is the n x n matrix with 1s on the diagonal and 0s elsewhere; AI = IA = A for any compatible A
Zero matrix: the matrix of all zeros; A + 0 = A; A * 0 = 0
Block matrices: partitioning large matrices into blocks that can be multiplied using the standard formula applied to blocks as if they were single entries
lecture_notes.demonstration: Visualize matrix-vector multiplication as a linear combination of columns in 2D. Given A = [[1, 2], [0, 1]] and x = [3, 1], show that Ax = 3 * [1, 0] + 1 * [2, 1] = [5, 1], plotting the column vectors and their weighted sum. Then multiply two 2x2 transformation matrices and show that AB != BA by applying each product to the same vector and comparing results. Use NumPy to verify all calculations.

review.value: Ax = x_1 * col_1(A) + x_2 * col_2(A) + ... This means Ax always lies in the column space of A: the span of A's columns. The equation Ax = b has a solution if and only if b is in the column space of A. This geometric insight is more powerful than just computing dot products.

A (m x n) times B (n x p) = C (m x p). The inner dimensions must match (n). The outer dimensions determine the result size (m x p). A common error is attempting to multiply matrices with incompatible dimensions.

In graphics, rotating then translating gives a different result than translating then rotating. In neural networks, the order of layer weights matters. In quantum computing, non-commuting operators correspond to incompatible measurements. Always respect the order of matrix multiplication.

Writing a system of m equations in n unknowns as Ax = b is more than notational convenience. It reveals the structure: A describes the linear relationships, x is the unknown vector, and b is the observation. Solving systems, least squares regression, and optimization all start from this formulation.

learning_objectives: Determine whether a square matrix is invertible using multiple criteria
Compute the inverse of a matrix using the augmented matrix method [A | I] -> [I | A^(-1)]
Apply the inverse to solve systems of equations: x = A^(-1)b
Understand why non-square matrices and singular square matrices are not invertible

lecture_notes.introduction: The inverse of a matrix A, denoted A^(-1), is the unique matrix such that AA^(-1) = A^(-1)A = I. The inverse undoes the transformation represented by A: if A rotates vectors by 30 degrees, then A^(-1) rotates them by -30 degrees. If A is invertible, the system Ax = b has exactly one solution for every b, given by x = A^(-1)b. However, not every matrix has an inverse. A matrix is singular (non-invertible) if and only if its rows or columns are linearly dependent, which means the transformation collapses some dimension. A 2x2 matrix that maps all of R^2 onto a line has lost a dimension of information and cannot be inverted. The conditions for invertibility form one of the most important theorems in linear algebra, connecting determinants, rank, null space, eigenvalues, and column independence into a single equivalence. Computing the inverse by row-reducing [A | I] to [I | A^(-1)] is an elegant application of Gaussian elimination. In practice, however, computing A^(-1) to solve Ax = b is less efficient and less numerically stable than directly applying Gaussian elimination to [A | b]. Libraries like NumPy use LU decomposition rather than explicit inverse computation.
key_concepts: Definition: A^(-1) is the unique matrix such that AA^(-1) = A^(-1)A = I; only square matrices can have inverses

Invertible Matrix Theorem: the following are equivalent for an n x n matrix A: A is invertible, det(A) != 0, rank(A) = n, the null space of A is {0}, the columns of A are linearly independent, Ax = b has exactly one solution for every b, all eigenvalues are nonzero
2x2 inverse formula: for A = [[a, b], [c, d]], A^(-1) = (1/(ad-bc)) * [[d, -b], [-c, a]]; exists if and only if ad - bc != 0
General inverse computation: row reduce [A | I_n] to [I_n | A^(-1)] using Gauss-Jordan elimination; if A does not reduce to I, then A is singular
Properties of inverses: (A^(-1))^(-1) = A, (AB)^(-1) = B^(-1)A^(-1) (note reversal), (A^T)^(-1) = (A^(-1))^T
Solving systems with the inverse: if A is invertible, x = A^(-1)b gives the unique solution; however, direct Gaussian elimination on [A | b] is computationally cheaper
Singular matrices: A is singular when its rows/columns are linearly dependent; the transformation A collapses at least one dimension, losing information that cannot be recovered
Numerical considerations: computing A^(-1) requires approximately 2n^3 operations and amplifies numerical errors; LU decomposition or QR factorization is preferred for solving systems
Elementary matrices: each row operation corresponds to multiplication by an elementary matrix E; A^(-1) = E_k ... E_2 E_1 where the E_i are the elementary matrices from row reducing A to I
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute the inverse of a 3x3 matrix by row reducing [A | I] to [I | A^(-1)], showing each step. Verify by computing AA^(-1) = I. Then attempt to invert a singular matrix and show that the row reduction produces a row of zeros in the left half, proving non-invertibility. Compare hand computation with numpy.linalg.inv() and show timing differences for larger matrices.

review.value: For A = [[a, b], [c, d]], A^(-1) = (1/det(A)) * [[d, -b], [-c, a]] where det(A) = ad - bc. If det(A) = 0, the matrix is singular. The formula swaps the diagonal entries, negates the off-diagonal entries, and divides by the determinant.

For an n x n matrix, these are all equivalent: invertible, full rank, trivial null space, linearly independent columns, nonzero determinant, all nonzero eigenvalues. Showing any one of these proves all the others. This theorem is the central unifying result of the course.

Although x = A^(-1)b is elegant, computing A^(-1) requires ~2n^3 operations while solving Ax = b by Gaussian elimination requires ~(2/3)n^3. If you need to solve Ax = b for multiple different b vectors, LU decomposition is more efficient than computing the full inverse.

A singular 3x3 matrix maps all of R^3 onto a plane or line (losing one or more dimensions). Since information is lost, the transformation cannot be undone. This is why the inverse does not exist: you cannot recover the original 3D point from its image on the plane.

learning_objectives: Factor a matrix into lower and upper triangular factors A = LU
Solve systems efficiently using forward and back substitution with LU factors
Apply LU decomposition to solve multiple systems with the same coefficient matrix
Understand when LU decomposition exists and when pivoting is required (PA = LU)

lecture_notes.introduction: LU decomposition factors a matrix A into the product of a lower triangular matrix L and an upper triangular matrix U: A = LU. This factorization is the computational backbone of practical linear algebra. Every time NumPy, MATLAB, or LAPACK solves a system of equations, it first computes the LU decomposition. The key advantage is efficiency when solving multiple systems with the same coefficient matrix. Gaussian elimination on Ax = b costs (2/3)n^3 operations. If you need to solve Ax = b for 100 different right-hand sides b, repeating Gaussian elimination costs 100 * (2/3)n^3 operations. With LU decomposition, you factor A once at cost (2/3)n^3, then solve each Ly = b (forward substitution, cost n^2) and Ux = y (back substitution, cost n^2) for just n^2 per right-hand side. For 100 systems, the total cost is (2/3)n^3 + 100 * 2n^2, a dramatic saving. LU decomposition also provides the determinant for free (det(A) = product of diagonal entries of U) and the inverse efficiently (solve AX = I column by column). The factorization exists when no row swaps are needed during Gaussian elimination. When row swaps are needed, we use the PLU decomposition: PA = LU, where P is a permutation matrix that records the row swaps.
key_concepts: Lower triangular matrix L: all entries above the diagonal are zero; diagonal entries are 1 (unit lower triangular); below-diagonal entries are the multipliers from Gaussian elimination
Upper triangular matrix U: all entries below the diagonal are zero; U is the row echelon form of A produced by Gaussian elimination
LU factorization procedure: perform Gaussian elimination without row swaps; U is the resulting REF; L contains the negated multipliers in the appropriate positions with 1s on the diagonal
Solving Ax = b with LU: first solve Ly = b by forward substitution (top to bottom), then solve Ux = y by back substitution (bottom to top)
Efficiency: factoring A = LU costs (2/3)n^3; each subsequent solve costs 2n^2; critical for applications that solve many systems with the same A
PLU decomposition: when row swaps are needed, PA = LU where P is a permutation matrix; scipy.linalg.lu computes this automatically
Determinant from LU: det(A) = det(L) * det(U) = 1 * product of diagonal entries of U (adjusted by sign if P has an odd number of swaps)
Cholesky decomposition: for symmetric positive definite matrices, the special factorization A = LL^T is more efficient (half the cost) and always exists without pivoting
Practical importance: LAPACK's dgesv routine (used by NumPy, MATLAB, R, Julia) implements LU decomposition with partial pivoting as its primary system solver
lecture_notes.demonstration: Factor a 4x4 matrix into LU by performing Gaussian elimination and recording the multipliers. Verify that L * U = A. Then solve Ax = b using forward and back substitution. Solve a second system Ax = b' using the same L and U factors, showing the efficiency gain. Compare with scipy.linalg.lu_factor and scipy.linalg.lu_solve to verify.
setup_requirements: Python with SciPy for lu_factor and lu_solve
Timing comparison script for multiple right-hand sides

review.value: L records the multipliers used during Gaussian elimination. If you subtracted 3 times row 1 from row 2, then L(2,1) = 3. U is the row echelon form. Together, they encode the entire elimination process, allowing you to replay it efficiently for new right-hand sides.

LU decomposition without pivoting fails when a zero pivot is encountered (cannot divide by zero). Row swapping fixes this, giving PA = LU. In practice, partial pivoting is always used for numerical stability, even when no zero pivot exists, to avoid dividing by small numbers.

For symmetric positive definite matrices (covariance matrices, Gram matrices), the Cholesky decomposition A = LL^T is twice as fast as LU, requires half the storage, and is always numerically stable without pivoting. Use numpy.linalg.cholesky when the matrix is SPD.

In finite element analysis, circuit simulation, and optimization, the coefficient matrix A remains the same while the right-hand side b changes at each time step or iteration. LU decomposition of A is computed once, and each new b is solved in O(n^2) time. This is orders of magnitude faster than recomputing from scratch.

learning_objectives: Compute determinants of 2x2 and 3x3 matrices using the formulas and cofactor expansion
Apply row operations to simplify determinant computation for larger matrices
Interpret the determinant geometrically as a signed volume scaling factor
Connect the determinant to invertibility, eigenvalues, and system solvability

lecture_notes.introduction: The determinant is a single number that captures essential information about a square matrix. Geometrically, the determinant of a 2x2 matrix is the signed area of the parallelogram formed by its column vectors. For a 3x3 matrix, it is the signed volume of the parallelepiped. The sign indicates whether the transformation preserves or reverses orientation. A determinant of zero means the transformation collapses at least one dimension: the parallelogram degenerates to a line segment, or the parallelepiped flattens to a plane. This directly connects to invertibility: a matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is nonzero. The determinant also appears in Cramer's rule for solving systems, in the change-of-variables formula for multiple integrals, and in the characteristic polynomial for eigenvalues. Despite its theoretical importance, computing determinants via cofactor expansion is impractical for large matrices because it requires O(n!) operations. In practice, the determinant is computed from the LU decomposition in O(n^3) time: det(A) = product of the diagonal entries of U, adjusted for the sign of the permutation. Understanding what the determinant means is more important than computing it by hand for large matrices.
key_concepts: 2x2 determinant: det([[a, b], [c, d]]) = ad - bc; geometrically, the signed area of the parallelogram formed by the column vectors

3x3 determinant by cofactor expansion: expand along any row or column; det(A) = sum of a_ij * C_ij where C_ij = (-1)^(i+j) * M_ij is the cofactor and M_ij is the minor (determinant of the submatrix with row i and column j removed)
Properties of determinants: det(AB) = det(A) * det(B); det(A^T) = det(A); det(cA) = c^n * det(A) for an n x n matrix; swapping rows negates the determinant; a row of zeros means det = 0

Row reduction for determinants: row operations simplify computation: swapping rows changes sign, multiplying a row by c multiplies det by c, adding a multiple of one row to another does not change det; reduce to triangular form where det = product of diagonal entries
Geometric interpretation: |det(A)| is the volume scaling factor of the linear transformation A; det(A) > 0 preserves orientation, det(A) < 0 reverses orientation, det(A) = 0 collapses a dimension
Determinant and invertibility: A is invertible if and only if det(A) != 0; this is one condition in the Invertible Matrix Theorem
Cramer's rule: for Ax = b with invertible A, x_i = det(A_i) / det(A) where A_i has column i replaced by b; elegant but computationally expensive (O(n * n!) by cofactors)
Characteristic polynomial: det(A - lambda * I) = 0 defines the eigenvalues; the determinant connects to the eigenvalue problem
Computational cost: cofactor expansion is O(n!); LU-based computation is O(n^3); always use LU for numerical computation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute the determinant of a 3x3 matrix by cofactor expansion along the first row. Then compute the same determinant by row reducing to upper triangular form and taking the product of diagonal entries, showing both methods give the same answer. Visualize the 2x2 case: plot the column vectors and the parallelogram, and show that the area equals |det(A)|. Then scale one column by 2 and show the area doubles, confirming det(2 col version) = 2 * det(original).

review.value: det([[a, b], [c, d]]) = ad - bc. The absolute value |ad - bc| is the area of the parallelogram formed by vectors [a, c] and [b, d]. If the determinant is positive, the second vector is counterclockwise from the first; if negative, clockwise. If zero, the vectors are parallel (no area).

det(AB) = det(A) * det(B). This means the volume scaling of a composed transformation is the product of the individual scalings. If A doubles volume and B triples volume, AB scales volume by 6. If either A or B has det = 0 (collapses a dimension), the product does too.

Adding a multiple of one row to another does not change the determinant (the most common row operation in Gaussian elimination). Swapping rows negates the determinant. Multiplying a row by c multiplies the determinant by c. Use these rules to compute determinants efficiently by row reduction.

In the Jacobian determinant for change of variables in integrals. In testing linear independence (nonzero determinant = independent). In the cross product formula (3x3 determinant). In testing whether a set of points is coplanar. In cryptography (determinant over finite fields for Hill cipher invertibility).

learning_objectives: Verify whether a set with given operations satisfies the vector space axioms
Identify the four fundamental subspaces of a matrix: column space, null space, row space, and left null space
Determine whether a subset of a vector space is a subspace
Recognize vector spaces beyond R^n including function spaces and polynomial spaces

lecture_notes.introduction: Vector spaces provide the abstract framework that unifies all of linear algebra. So far, we have worked with vectors in R^n, the space of n-tuples of real numbers. But the same algebraic structure appears in many other settings: the set of all polynomials of degree at most n, the set of all continuous functions on an interval, the set of all m x n matrices, and even the set of solutions to a homogeneous differential equation. What these sets have in common is that they are closed under addition and scalar multiplication and satisfy a set of axioms (associativity, commutativity, distributivity, existence of zero vector and additive inverses). Recognizing a set as a vector space means that all of linear algebra applies: we can talk about linear independence, basis, dimension, linear transformations, and eigenvalues in that space. Subspaces are vector spaces contained within larger vector spaces. The four fundamental subspaces of a matrix (column space, null space, row space, and left null space) are the key to understanding the structure of linear systems. The column space tells you which right-hand sides b make Ax = b solvable. The null space describes the family of solutions when solutions exist. Together, they decompose R^n in a way that reveals everything about the linear system.
key_concepts: Vector space axioms: closure under addition and scalar multiplication, associativity, commutativity of addition, additive identity (zero vector), additive inverses, distributivity, scalar multiplication identity

Examples of vector spaces: R^n, the space of m x n matrices, the space of polynomials of degree <= n (P_n), the space of continuous functions C[a,b], solution spaces of homogeneous linear differential equations
Subspace definition: a subset S of a vector space V is a subspace if (1) the zero vector is in S, (2) S is closed under addition, and (3) S is closed under scalar multiplication
Column space C(A): the span of the columns of A; the set of all possible outputs Ax; Ax = b is solvable if and only if b is in C(A)
Null space N(A): the set of all x such that Ax = 0; this is always a subspace of R^n; describes the 'lost information' or 'degrees of freedom' in the system
Row space C(A^T): the span of the rows of A; row reduction does not change the row space; the nonzero rows of the REF form a basis
Left null space N(A^T): the set of all y such that A^T y = 0; related to the column space by the fundamental theorem
Span: the set of all linear combinations of a given set of vectors; span(v_1, ..., v_k) is always a subspace
Fundamental theorem preview: C(A) and N(A^T) are orthogonal complements in R^m; C(A^T) and N(A) are orthogonal complements in R^n
lecture_notes.demonstration: Show that the set of solutions to a homogeneous system Ax = 0 is a subspace of R^n by verifying the three subspace conditions. Compute the column space and null space of a 3x4 matrix by row reducing and identifying pivot and free columns. Show that a non-homogeneous solution set (Ax = b with b != 0) is NOT a subspace because it does not contain the zero vector. Visualize in R^3: the column space as a plane and the null space as a line through the origin, perpendicular to the row space.

review.value: C(A) = {Ax : x in R^n} is the set of all possible outputs of the transformation A. If A is 3x4, then C(A) is a subspace of R^3 with dimension equal to the rank of A. The equation Ax = b has a solution if and only if b lies in this subspace.

N(A) = {x : Ax = 0} is the set of all inputs that A maps to zero. If N(A) = {0}, then A maps distinct inputs to distinct outputs (injective). If N(A) is nontrivial, then infinitely many inputs map to the same output, and the general solution to Ax = b is x = x_particular + x_null for any x_null in N(A).

Instead of checking all axioms, a nonempty subset S is a subspace if and only if for all u, v in S and all scalars c: u + v is in S and cu is in S. Equivalently, check that cu + v is in S for all u, v in S and scalars c (one combined check).

P_3 (polynomials of degree at most 3) is a 4-dimensional vector space with basis {1, x, x^2, x^3}. The derivative operator D: P_3 -> P_2 is a linear transformation. Its null space is the constant polynomials. All the same concepts apply: dimension, basis, null space, range.

learning_objectives: Determine whether a set of vectors forms a basis for a given vector space
Find a basis for the column space, null space, and row space of a matrix
Compute the rank and nullity of a matrix and verify the rank-nullity theorem
Change coordinates between different bases

lecture_notes.introduction: A basis is a set of vectors that is both linearly independent and spans the vector space. It is the minimal complete description of the space: removing any vector from the basis leaves a set that no longer spans, and adding any vector creates a linearly dependent set. The number of vectors in any basis is the dimension of the space, and this number is the same regardless of which basis you choose. Dimension is the most fundamental invariant of a vector space: R^3 always has dimension 3, the space of 2x2 matrices has dimension 4, and the space of polynomials of degree at most n has dimension n+1. For matrices, the rank is the dimension of the column space (equivalently, the row space), and the nullity is the dimension of the null space. The rank-nullity theorem states that rank + nullity = number of columns, connecting the dimensions of the column space and null space in a precise way. This theorem has profound implications: it tells you that information preserved by the transformation (rank) plus information lost (nullity) equals the total information in the input. Finding bases for the fundamental subspaces of a matrix is one of the most important computational skills in linear algebra, and it connects directly to practical applications like identifying independent features in a dataset or determining the number of free parameters in a system.
key_concepts: Linear independence: vectors v_1, ..., v_k are linearly independent if the only solution to c_1*v_1 + ... + c_k*v_k = 0 is c_1 = ... = c_k = 0; no vector can be written as a combination of the others
Span: the set of all linear combinations of a given set of vectors; span(S) is the smallest subspace containing all vectors in S
Basis: a linearly independent set that spans the vector space; every vector in the space can be written uniquely as a linear combination of basis vectors
Dimension: the number of vectors in any basis; all bases of the same space have the same number of vectors (this is a theorem, not a definition)
Rank of A: the dimension of the column space of A; equals the number of pivot positions in the REF of A
Nullity of A: the dimension of the null space of A; equals the number of free variables; equals the number of non-pivot columns
Rank-nullity theorem: rank(A) + nullity(A) = number of columns of A; the total dimension of the domain is split between the image and the kernel
Finding basis for column space: the pivot columns of A (not of the REF) form a basis for C(A)
Coordinate vectors: if B = {b_1, ..., b_n} is a basis, the coordinate vector [x]_B expresses x as a linear combination of basis vectors; change of basis is a matrix multiplication
lecture_notes.demonstration: For a 4x5 matrix A: compute the RREF, identify pivot and free columns, extract the basis for the column space (pivot columns of A), the basis for the null space (from the free variable parameterization), and the basis for the row space (nonzero rows of the RREF). Verify rank + nullity = 5. Then demonstrate change of basis: given a non-standard basis B for R^2, find the change-of-basis matrix and convert coordinates.
setup_requirements: Visualization for basis vectors in R^2/R^3

review.value: The rank of a data matrix tells you the number of truly independent features. If a 100-feature dataset has rank 10, there are only 10 independent dimensions of variation, and the other 90 features are linear combinations of these 10. PCA finds these independent dimensions.

For A with n columns: rank(A) + nullity(A) = n. The rank counts preserved dimensions; the nullity counts lost dimensions. A 5x3 matrix has at most rank 3. If rank = 2, then nullity = 1, meaning a 1-dimensional family of inputs maps to zero (and the output is 2-dimensional).

From the RREF, set each free variable to 1 (others to 0) one at a time and solve for the basic variables. Each choice gives one null space basis vector. The number of basis vectors equals the number of free variables (the nullity).

Two vector spaces are isomorphic (structurally identical) if and only if they have the same dimension. R^3, the space of 3x1 column vectors, and the space of polynomials of degree at most 2 are all 3-dimensional and therefore isomorphic. Dimension completely classifies finite-dimensional vector spaces.

learning_objectives: Determine whether a function between vector spaces is a linear transformation
Find the standard matrix of a linear transformation from R^n to R^m
Analyze the kernel and range of a linear transformation
Compose linear transformations and represent the composition as a matrix product

lecture_notes.introduction: A linear transformation is a function T: V -> W between vector spaces that preserves the operations of addition and scalar multiplication: T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v). These two conditions have far-reaching consequences: linear transformations map the zero vector to the zero vector, preserve linear combinations, and map subspaces to subspaces. Every linear transformation from R^n to R^m can be represented as multiplication by an m x n matrix, and every such matrix defines a linear transformation. This correspondence between transformations and matrices is the bridge between the abstract theory and practical computation. The matrix representation of a transformation depends on the choice of basis: the same geometric rotation has different matrix representations in different coordinate systems. This is not a bug but a feature: choosing the right basis can simplify the matrix dramatically, which is the key idea behind diagonalization and the spectral theorem. The kernel (null space) of a transformation is the set of inputs mapped to zero, and the range (image) is the set of all possible outputs. Together, they determine whether the transformation is injective, surjective, or both, which controls whether equations T(x) = b have unique solutions, infinitely many solutions, or no solution.
key_concepts: Linear transformation definition: T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cu) = cT(u) for all vectors u, v and scalars c; equivalently, T(cu + v) = cT(u) + T(v)
Standard matrix: if T: R^n -> R^m, the standard matrix A has columns A = [T(e_1) | T(e_2) | ... | T(e_n)]; then T(x) = Ax for all x
Examples of linear transformations: rotation, reflection, projection, differentiation of polynomials, integration (definite integral as a linear functional)
Non-examples: translation (T(x) = x + b is not linear because T(0) != 0), squaring (T(x) = x^2 is not linear because T(2x) = 4x^2 != 2x^2 = 2T(x))
Kernel (null space): ker(T) = {x : T(x) = 0}; always a subspace of the domain; T is injective if and only if ker(T) = {0}
Range (image): range(T) = {T(x) : x in domain}; always a subspace of the codomain; T is surjective if and only if range(T) = codomain
Rank-nullity for transformations: dim(ker(T)) + dim(range(T)) = dim(domain)
Composition of transformations: if T: R^n -> R^m and S: R^m -> R^p, then S composed with T is represented by the matrix product [S][T]
Invertible transformations: T is invertible if and only if its matrix is invertible; the inverse transformation is represented by the inverse matrix
lecture_notes.demonstration: Show that the rotation transformation R_theta: R^2 -> R^2 is linear by verifying the axioms, then derive the rotation matrix [[cos theta, -sin theta], [sin theta, cos theta]] by computing where e_1 and e_2 map. Compose rotation by 30 degrees with rotation by 45 degrees and show the result is rotation by 75 degrees, verifying through matrix multiplication. Then show that translation T(x) = x + [1, 0] is NOT linear (T(0) = [1, 0] != 0) and discuss how affine transformations extend linear transformations.
setup_requirements: Python with NumPy and matplotlib for transformation visualization
2D grid visualization for showing how transformations distort the plane

review.value: To find the matrix of T: R^n -> R^m, compute T(e_1), T(e_2), ..., T(e_n) and arrange these as columns. This works because T(x) = T(x_1*e_1 + ... + x_n*e_n) = x_1*T(e_1) + ... + x_n*T(e_n) = Ax by linearity.

If ker(T) = {0}, then T is injective (one-to-one), meaning T(x) = b has at most one solution. If ker(T) is nontrivial, then the equation T(x) = b has either no solution or infinitely many solutions (differing by elements of the kernel).

Applying T then S to x gives S(T(x)) = [S]([T]x) = ([S][T])x. The matrix of the composition is the product of the matrices. This is why matrix multiplication is defined the way it is: it corresponds to function composition.

Linear transformations of R^2 map lines to lines (or points), parallelograms to parallelograms, and the origin to itself. They are completely determined by where they send the two basis vectors. This is why the matrix has two columns: one for each basis vector's image.

learning_objectives: Compute eigenvalues by solving the characteristic polynomial det(A - lambda * I) = 0
Find eigenvectors for each eigenvalue by solving (A - lambda * I)x = 0
Interpret eigenvalues and eigenvectors geometrically as stretching directions and factors
Apply eigenanalysis to understand dynamical systems, stability, and growth

lecture_notes.introduction: Eigenvectors are the directions along which a linear transformation acts by simple scaling: Av = lambda * v, where v is the eigenvector and lambda is the eigenvalue (the scaling factor). Most vectors change direction when multiplied by a matrix, but eigenvectors only change magnitude. This makes eigenvectors the natural coordinate system for the transformation. In the eigenvector basis, the matrix becomes diagonal, and all computations simplify dramatically. Eigenvalues and eigenvectors arise throughout science and engineering. In mechanical vibration analysis, eigenvalues determine the natural frequencies and eigenvectors determine the mode shapes. In population dynamics, the dominant eigenvalue determines the long-term growth rate. In Markov chains, the eigenvector with eigenvalue 1 is the steady-state probability distribution. In quantum mechanics, eigenvalues of operators are the measurable values of physical observables. In data science, the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix are the variances along the principal components (PCA). In Google's PageRank, the ranking is the dominant eigenvector of the web link matrix. The characteristic polynomial det(A - lambda * I) = 0 is the standard method for computing eigenvalues of small matrices by hand, though numerical methods (QR algorithm) are used for large matrices.
key_concepts: Eigenvalue equation: Av = lambda * v, where v != 0; the eigenvector v is scaled by factor lambda under the transformation A
Characteristic polynomial: p(lambda) = det(A - lambda * I); the eigenvalues are the roots of this polynomial
Computing eigenvalues: set det(A - lambda * I) = 0, expand the determinant, and solve the resulting polynomial equation
Computing eigenvectors: for each eigenvalue lambda_i, solve (A - lambda_i * I)v = 0 to find the corresponding eigenspace (null space of A - lambda_i * I)
Eigenspace: the set of all eigenvectors for a given eigenvalue plus the zero vector; always a subspace of R^n
Algebraic multiplicity: the multiplicity of lambda as a root of the characteristic polynomial; geometric multiplicity: the dimension of the eigenspace; geometric <= algebraic
Trace and determinant from eigenvalues: trace(A) = sum of eigenvalues; det(A) = product of eigenvalues
Complex eigenvalues: real matrices can have complex eigenvalues, which come in conjugate pairs a +/- bi; correspond to rotation-scaling behavior
Application: dynamical systems x(t+1) = Ax(t); the eigenvalues determine stability (all |lambda_i| < 1 implies convergence to zero) and growth (any |lambda_i| > 1 implies divergence)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A = [[4, 2], [1, 3]] step by step. Characteristic polynomial: (4-lambda)(3-lambda) - 2 = lambda^2 - 7*lambda + 10 = (lambda-5)(lambda-2) = 0. Eigenvalues: 5 and 2. Eigenvectors: for lambda=5, v = [2, 1]; for lambda=2, v = [-1, 1]. Visualize: plot the eigenvectors and show that A stretches the [2,1] direction by 5 and the [-1,1] direction by 2. Plot several other vectors and show they change direction, but the eigenvectors stay on their lines.
setup_requirements: 2D visualization of eigenvectors and transformed vectors

review.value: Think of eigenvalues as the 'stretching factors' of the transformation along its natural directions (eigenvectors). A transformation with eigenvalues 3 and 0.5 stretches space by 3 in one direction and compresses by half in another. Eigenvalue 0 means the transformation collapses that direction to zero (the matrix is singular).

PCA computes the eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix. The eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue points in the direction of maximum variance (most information). Projecting onto the top k eigenvectors reduces dimensionality while preserving the most information.

For a discrete dynamical system x(t+1) = Ax(t), the long-term behavior is determined by the eigenvalues. If all eigenvalues have |lambda| < 1, the system converges to zero (stable). If any eigenvalue has |lambda| > 1, the system diverges (unstable). If the largest eigenvalue is exactly 1, the system approaches a steady state (Markov chains).

A 2x2 rotation matrix has complex eigenvalues cos(theta) +/- i*sin(theta). The absolute value |lambda| = 1 (no stretching), and the angle of the complex eigenvalue equals the rotation angle. Complex eigenvalues always indicate rotational behavior in the transformation.

learning_objectives: Determine whether a matrix is diagonalizable based on its eigenvalues and eigenvectors
Diagonalize a matrix A = PDP^(-1) and explain the meaning of each factor
Use diagonalization to compute matrix powers A^k efficiently
Apply diagonalization to solve linear recurrences and dynamical systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Diagonalization is the process of changing to the eigenvector basis where the matrix becomes diagonal. If A has n linearly independent eigenvectors, we can write A = PDP^(-1), where P is the matrix whose columns are the eigenvectors and D is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues. In the eigenvector basis, the transformation A simply scales each coordinate independently by the corresponding eigenvalue. This simplification makes many computations trivial. Matrix powers become: A^k = PD^kP^(-1), where D^k simply raises each diagonal entry to the k-th power. This transforms the computation of A^k from k matrix multiplications to a single diagonalization plus scalar exponentiation. Applications include solving linear recurrence relations (the Fibonacci sequence is a diagonalization problem), analyzing discrete dynamical systems, computing matrix exponentials for continuous systems, and understanding Markov chain convergence. Not every matrix is diagonalizable: a matrix is diagonalizable if and only if it has n linearly independent eigenvectors, which happens when the geometric multiplicity of each eigenvalue equals its algebraic multiplicity. Defective matrices (non-diagonalizable) require the Jordan normal form, which we touch on briefly.
key_concepts: Diagonalization: A = PDP^(-1) where P = [v_1 | v_2 | ... | v_n] (eigenvectors as columns) and D = diag(lambda_1, ..., lambda_n) (eigenvalues on the diagonal)
Diagonalizability criterion: A is diagonalizable if and only if it has n linearly independent eigenvectors; sufficient (not necessary) condition: n distinct eigenvalues
Matrix powers: A^k = PD^kP^(-1); D^k = diag(lambda_1^k, ..., lambda_n^k); reduces matrix exponentiation to scalar exponentiation

Fibonacci via diagonalization: the recurrence F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) can be written as a matrix equation [F(n+1), F(n)]^T = A^n [F(1), F(0)]^T where A = [[1,1],[1,0]]; diagonalizing A gives the closed form F(n) = (phi^n - psi^n)/sqrt(5)
Dynamical systems: for x(t+1) = Ax(t), diagonalization gives x(t) = PD^t P^(-1) x(0); each component of the solution in the eigenvector basis evolves as c_i * lambda_i^t
Markov chain convergence: for a regular stochastic matrix, the steady-state vector is the eigenvector for eigenvalue 1; diagonalization shows convergence rate depends on the second-largest eigenvalue

Defective matrices: matrices without n linearly independent eigenvectors cannot be diagonalized; the Jordan normal form provides the next best thing (block diagonal with 1s above the diagonal in each block)
Symmetric matrices are always diagonalizable: real symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors; A = QDQ^T where Q is orthogonal (the spectral theorem)
Similarity: A and PDP^(-1) are similar matrices representing the same transformation in different bases; similar matrices have the same eigenvalues, determinant, trace, and rank
lecture_notes.demonstration: Diagonalize A = [[4, 2], [1, 3]] using the eigenvalues (5, 2) and eigenvectors ([2,1], [-1,1]) found previously. Construct P = [[2, -1], [1, 1]] and D = [[5, 0], [0, 2]]. Verify A = PDP^(-1) by matrix multiplication. Then compute A^10 using A^10 = PD^10P^(-1) = P * [[5^10, 0], [0, 2^10]] * P^(-1) and verify with NumPy. Apply to the Fibonacci sequence: diagonalize [[1,1],[1,0]] to derive the closed-form Fibonacci formula and verify for n = 1 through 10.
setup_requirements: Python with NumPy for verification and large power computation

review.value: In the eigenvector basis, A is diagonal. Diagonal matrices are trivial to raise to powers, invert, and analyze. A = PDP^(-1) means: change to eigenvector basis (P^(-1)), apply the simple diagonal transformation (D), change back to the original basis (P).

If an n x n matrix has n distinct eigenvalues, it is guaranteed to be diagonalizable. This is because eigenvectors from distinct eigenvalues are automatically linearly independent. The converse is false: a matrix can be diagonalizable even with repeated eigenvalues (e.g., the identity matrix has eigenvalue 1 repeated n times and is trivially diagonal).

F(n) = (phi^n - psi^n)/sqrt(5) where phi = (1+sqrt(5))/2 (golden ratio) and psi = (1-sqrt(5))/2. Since |psi| < 1, psi^n -> 0, so F(n) is approximately phi^n / sqrt(5) for large n. This exact formula comes from diagonalizing the Fibonacci recurrence matrix.

Real symmetric matrices are always diagonalizable with real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors. This is the spectral theorem, the most important theorem in applied linear algebra. Every covariance matrix is symmetric, so PCA always works.

learning_objectives: Define and compute inner products, norms, and distances in R^n
Determine orthogonality and compute angles between vectors
Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz and triangle inequalities
Recognize inner product spaces beyond R^n including function spaces

lecture_notes.introduction: Up to this point, we have studied vector spaces purely in terms of addition and scalar multiplication. Inner product spaces add the concept of angle and length, enabling us to measure distances, determine orthogonality, and define projections. The standard inner product on R^n is the dot product: <u, v> = u^T v = sum of u_i * v_i. This gives us the Euclidean norm ||v|| = sqrt(<v, v>) and the angle between vectors through cos(theta) = <u, v> / (||u|| * ||v||). Two vectors are orthogonal when their inner product is zero, meaning they are perpendicular. Inner products are not limited to R^n. The inner product on the space of continuous functions on [0, 1] is <f, g> = integral from 0 to 1 of f(x)g(x) dx. Fourier analysis decomposes functions into orthogonal components (sines and cosines) using this inner product, exactly as we decompose vectors into orthogonal components in R^n. The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality |<u, v>| <= ||u|| * ||v|| is the fundamental inequality of inner product spaces, and it has profound consequences including the triangle inequality and the validity of the cosine formula for angles. Understanding inner products is essential for the orthogonality and least squares applications that follow in the next sessions.
key_concepts: Dot product (standard inner product): <u, v> = u^T v = u_1*v_1 + u_2*v_2 + ... + u_n*v_n; a scalar that measures alignment between vectors
Norm (length): ||v|| = sqrt(<v, v>) = sqrt(v_1^2 + v_2^2 + ... + v_n^2); the Euclidean length of the vector
Distance: d(u, v) = ||u - v||; the Euclidean distance between the endpoints of vectors u and v
Angle: cos(theta) = <u, v> / (||u|| * ||v||); valid because Cauchy-Schwarz guarantees the fraction is between -1 and 1
Orthogonality: u and v are orthogonal if <u, v> = 0; geometrically perpendicular; extends to more than two vectors (mutually orthogonal set)
Cauchy-Schwarz inequality: |<u, v>| <= ||u|| * ||v||; equality holds if and only if u and v are parallel (one is a scalar multiple of the other)
Triangle inequality: ||u + v|| <= ||u|| + ||v||; the length of a sum is at most the sum of the lengths; consequence of Cauchy-Schwarz
Unit vectors: vectors with ||v|| = 1; normalizing: v/||v|| produces a unit vector in the same direction
Inner product on function spaces: <f, g> = integral of f(x)g(x) dx; orthogonal functions satisfy <f, g> = 0; sin(nx) and cos(mx) are orthogonal on [0, 2*pi] for n != m
Cosine similarity: <u, v> / (||u|| * ||v||); used in information retrieval and NLP to measure document similarity through term-frequency vectors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute the dot product, norms, angle, and distance for several vector pairs in R^3. Show that [1, 0, 1] and [0, 1, 0] are orthogonal. Demonstrate the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality with numerical examples and show when equality holds (parallel vectors). Then compute the inner product of f(x) = sin(x) and g(x) = cos(x) on [0, 2*pi] and show it equals zero (orthogonal functions). Connect to NLP: compute cosine similarity between document term-frequency vectors.
setup_requirements: Function plotting tool for inner product visualization

review.value: <u, v> is positive when the vectors point in roughly the same direction (angle < 90 degrees), zero when perpendicular (angle = 90 degrees), and negative when they point in roughly opposite directions (angle > 90 degrees). The magnitude |<u, v>| is largest when the vectors are parallel.

Orthogonal vectors are independent in the strongest sense: not only linearly independent, but also 'non-interfering.' Projecting onto orthogonal directions gives independent components. This is why orthogonal bases are preferred: they decompose vectors into non-overlapping contributions.

In statistics: the correlation coefficient is |<x, y>| / (||x|| * ||y||) <= 1 by Cauchy-Schwarz. In signal processing: the matched filter output is bounded by Cauchy-Schwarz. In optimization: many bounds on optimization algorithms rely on Cauchy-Schwarz.

In NLP, documents are represented as term-frequency vectors. Cosine similarity = <d1, d2> / (||d1|| * ||d2||) measures topic similarity regardless of document length. This is the basis for search engines and recommendation systems.

learning_objectives: Apply the Gram-Schmidt process to orthogonalize a set of vectors
Compute the QR factorization of a matrix
Project vectors onto subspaces using orthogonal projection
Apply orthogonal projection to solve least squares problems

lecture_notes.introduction: Orthogonal bases are the most useful bases in applied mathematics because they make projections trivial. If {q_1, ..., q_k} is an orthonormal basis for a subspace S, then the projection of any vector b onto S is simply proj_S(b) = <b, q_1>q_1 + <b, q_2>q_2 + ... + <b, q_k>q_k. No matrix inversion, no system solving, just dot products. The Gram-Schmidt process converts any linearly independent set of vectors into an orthonormal set spanning the same subspace. It works by subtracting from each vector its projection onto the previously orthogonalized vectors, leaving only the component orthogonal to all of them, and then normalizing. When applied to the columns of a matrix A, Gram-Schmidt produces the QR factorization: A = QR, where Q has orthonormal columns and R is upper triangular. QR is the preferred factorization for least squares problems and eigenvalue computation. Orthogonal projection is the mathematical foundation of least squares: when Ax = b has no exact solution, the best approximation x-hat minimizes ||Ax - b|| and satisfies A^T Ax-hat = A^T b (the normal equations). The solution x-hat produces the projection of b onto the column space of A. This is the mathematical basis of linear regression, the most widely used statistical method in science and engineering.
key_concepts: Orthogonal set: a set of vectors where every pair is orthogonal; orthonormal set: orthogonal set where every vector has unit length
Orthogonal projection onto a line: proj_u(v) = (<v, u> / <u, u>) * u; the component of v in the direction of u
Orthogonal projection onto a subspace: proj_S(b) = sum of <b, q_i> * q_i for an orthonormal basis {q_i} of S; equivalently, proj_S(b) = QQ^T b where Q = [q_1 | ... | q_k]
Gram-Schmidt process: given linearly independent {v_1, ..., v_k}, produce orthonormal {q_1, ..., q_k} by iteratively: u_i = v_i - sum of proj_{q_j}(v_i) for j < i, then q_i = u_i / ||u_i||
QR factorization: A = QR where Q has orthonormal columns (Q^T Q = I) and R is upper triangular; computed by applying Gram-Schmidt to the columns of A
Least squares problem: find x-hat that minimizes ||Ax - b||^2 when Ax = b has no exact solution (more equations than unknowns, inconsistent system)
Normal equations: A^T A x-hat = A^T b; the solution x-hat gives the least squares best fit; if A has linearly independent columns, x-hat = (A^T A)^(-1) A^T b
QR solution of least squares: Rx-hat = Q^T b; more numerically stable than the normal equations because it avoids computing A^T A (which squares the condition number)
Linear regression as projection: fitting y = beta_0 + beta_1 * x to data points projects the observation vector y onto the column space of the design matrix X
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply Gram-Schmidt to three vectors in R^3 to produce an orthonormal basis. Verify orthonormality by computing all dot products. Then construct the QR factorization of a 4x3 matrix and verify A = QR. Solve a least squares problem: fit a line y = a + bx to 5 data points by setting up the normal equations, solving for the coefficients, and plotting the best-fit line against the data. Compare the normal equations solution with the QR solution.
setup_requirements: Data plotting tool for least squares visualization

review.value: Each step removes the components along previously orthogonalized directions, leaving only the new, independent direction. It is like building a coordinate system one axis at a time: each new axis is forced to be perpendicular to all existing axes. The result is the most 'independent' representation possible.

The normal equations A^T A x = A^T b require forming A^T A, which squares the condition number (doubles the number of digits lost to rounding). QR avoids this: Rx = Q^T b involves only the condition number of A itself. Always prefer QR for numerical computation; use normal equations only for theoretical derivation.

The least squares solution Ax-hat is the projection of b onto the column space of A. The residual b - Ax-hat is orthogonal to the column space. This geometric picture explains why A^T(b - Ax-hat) = 0 (the normal equations): the error vector is perpendicular to every column of A.

Linear regression y = X*beta + epsilon minimizes ||y - X*beta||^2. This is exactly the least squares problem. The solution beta-hat = (X^T X)^(-1) X^T y is the projection formula. The R^2 value measures what fraction of y's variance is captured by the projection onto C(X).

learning_objectives: Apply least squares to linear regression with multiple variables
Fit polynomial models using least squares with the Vandermonde matrix
Evaluate model quality using residual analysis and R-squared
Connect least squares to the pseudoinverse and minimum-norm solutions

lecture_notes.introduction: Least squares is the bridge between linear algebra and data science. Whenever you fit a model to data, you are (usually) solving a least squares problem. Simple linear regression, multiple regression, polynomial fitting, and many other statistical models reduce to finding the vector x that minimizes ||Ax - b||^2. The normal equations A^T Ax = A^T b provide the mathematical solution, but the computational implementation uses QR factorization or SVD for numerical stability. Understanding least squares at the linear algebra level gives you insight into what statistical software is actually doing: how R-squared relates to the projection onto the column space, why multicollinearity causes problems (A^T A becomes nearly singular), and how regularization (ridge regression) corresponds to adding a penalty term that makes the system better-conditioned. This session focuses on applications: fitting models to real data, evaluating the quality of the fit, understanding the geometry behind the statistical measures, and connecting to the pseudoinverse. The pseudoinverse A^+ generalizes the inverse to non-square and singular matrices, providing the minimum-norm least squares solution for any system, and it is the foundation for many machine learning algorithms.
key_concepts: Multiple linear regression: y = beta_0 + beta_1*x_1 + ... + beta_p*x_p expressed as y = X*beta; least squares gives beta-hat = (X^T X)^(-1) X^T y
Polynomial fitting: fitting y = a_0 + a_1*x + a_2*x^2 + ... + a_n*x^n; the design matrix is the Vandermonde matrix with rows [1, x_i, x_i^2, ..., x_i^n]
Residual vector: r = b - Ax-hat; the difference between observed and predicted values; orthogonal to C(A) by the normal equations
R-squared: R^2 = 1 - ||r||^2 / ||b - mean(b)||^2; the fraction of variance explained by the model; R^2 = 1 means perfect fit, R^2 = 0 means the model is no better than the mean
Overfitting: increasing polynomial degree always improves R^2 on training data but may worsen prediction on new data; the model fits noise rather than signal
Multicollinearity: when columns of X are nearly linearly dependent, X^T X is nearly singular and beta-hat has high variance; small changes in data cause large changes in coefficients
Ridge regression: minimize ||Ax - b||^2 + alpha * ||x||^2 to regularize the solution; the solution beta = (X^T X + alpha * I)^(-1) X^T y stabilizes the inverse
Pseudoinverse: A^+ = (A^T A)^(-1) A^T when A has full column rank; more generally, A^+ = V * Sigma^+ * U^T from the SVD; gives the minimum-norm least squares solution
Weighted least squares: minimize ||W(Ax - b)||^2 where W is a diagonal weight matrix; gives more influence to data points with higher weight (or lower variance)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Fit a polynomial of degree 1, 2, 3, and 10 to 15 data points. Show how the fit improves with degree (lower residuals) but degree 10 overfits wildly between data points. Compute R^2 for each fit and discuss the bias-variance trade-off. Then demonstrate multicollinearity: create two nearly identical features and show that the regression coefficients become huge and unstable. Apply ridge regression and show the coefficients stabilize.
setup_requirements: Python with NumPy, SciPy, and matplotlib

review.value: Increasing the polynomial degree increases the dimension of the column space. When the column space becomes high-dimensional enough to nearly contain the data vector b, the residual shrinks but the solution becomes wildly oscillatory. The projection is nearly perfect on the training data but meaningless for new data.

If two columns of X are nearly parallel, X^T X has a near-zero eigenvalue, making (X^T X)^(-1) have a huge eigenvalue. This amplifies noise in the data into wild swings in the coefficients. The condition number of X^T X measures this sensitivity: condition number >> 1 indicates multicollinearity.

Adding alpha * I to X^T X shifts all eigenvalues up by alpha, preventing near-zero eigenvalues. The trade-off: the solution is biased (systematically shrunk toward zero) but has lower variance. The optimal alpha balances bias and variance, typically chosen by cross-validation.

The pseudoinverse A^+ = V * Sigma^+ * U^T inverts the nonzero singular values and transposes. It gives the minimum-norm solution among all least squares solutions. When A is square and invertible, A^+ = A^(-1). When A is overdetermined (more rows than columns), A^+ gives the least squares solution.

learning_objectives: Compute the SVD of a matrix and interpret its components geometrically
Apply SVD to low-rank approximation and data compression
Connect SVD to PCA, the pseudoinverse, and the fundamental theorem of linear algebra
Evaluate the rank and condition number of a matrix using singular values

lecture_notes.introduction: The Singular Value Decomposition is the culmination of the course and arguably the most important matrix factorization in applied mathematics. Every matrix, regardless of shape or rank, has an SVD: A = U * Sigma * V^T, where U and V are orthogonal matrices and Sigma is a diagonal matrix of non-negative values called singular values. The SVD reveals everything about a matrix: its rank (number of nonzero singular values), its column and row spaces (columns of U and V), its null space (columns of V corresponding to zero singular values), its best low-rank approximation (truncate the smallest singular values), its condition number (ratio of largest to smallest singular value), and its pseudoinverse (invert the nonzero singular values). The Eckart-Young theorem guarantees that the best rank-k approximation of any matrix is obtained by keeping only the top k singular values and their corresponding vectors. This theorem underlies image compression (keeping the top singular values produces a compressed image), recommendation systems (collaborative filtering decomposes the rating matrix), natural language processing (latent semantic analysis), and dimensionality reduction (truncated SVD is equivalent to PCA when the data is centered). The SVD completes our journey from systems of equations to the deepest structure of linear transformations.
key_concepts: SVD definition: A = U * Sigma * V^T where U (m x m) and V (n x n) are orthogonal and Sigma (m x n) is diagonal with non-negative singular values sigma_1 >= sigma_2 >= ... >= 0
Computing SVD: singular values are square roots of eigenvalues of A^T A; V contains eigenvectors of A^T A; U contains eigenvectors of AA^T (or U = AV * Sigma^(-1) for nonzero singular values)
Geometric interpretation: every linear transformation can be decomposed as: rotate (V^T), scale along axes (Sigma), rotate again (U); this is the essence of SVD
Rank from SVD: rank(A) = number of nonzero singular values; the rank is exactly the number of independent 'pieces' of the transformation
Eckart-Young theorem: the best rank-k approximation of A (minimizing ||A - A_k|| in Frobenius or operator norm) is A_k = sum of sigma_i * u_i * v_i^T for i = 1 to k
Image compression: store an image as a matrix A, compute SVD, keep only top k singular values; the compressed representation uses 2k(m+n) numbers instead of mn
PCA via SVD: for centered data matrix X, the SVD X = U * Sigma * V^T gives principal components as columns of V, and the explained variance ratios as sigma_i^2 / sum(sigma_j^2)
Condition number: kappa(A) = sigma_max / sigma_min; measures how sensitive the solution of Ax = b is to perturbations in A or b; large condition number means ill-conditioned
Pseudoinverse from SVD: A^+ = V * Sigma^+ * U^T where Sigma^+ inverts the nonzero singular values and transposes; gives the minimum-norm least squares solution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute the SVD of a 3x2 matrix by hand (finding eigenvalues of A^T A, computing V, then U). Verify A = U * Sigma * V^T. Then compress a grayscale image using SVD: load the image as a matrix, compute the SVD, reconstruct at rank k = 1, 5, 20, 50, 100, and show the visual quality improving. Plot the singular value spectrum to show rapid decay (most information in the first few singular values). Compute the compression ratio at each rank.
setup_requirements: Python with NumPy (numpy.linalg.svd) and PIL for image loading
Grayscale test image for compression demonstration
matplotlib for singular value spectrum and image display

review.value: The best rank-k approximation of a matrix (minimizing the Frobenius norm of the error) is obtained by keeping only the top k singular values and their corresponding singular vectors. SVD gives the optimal compression for any matrix. No other rank-k matrix is closer to A.

Collaborative filtering decomposes the user-item rating matrix using SVD. The latent factors (singular vectors) capture hidden preferences like genre taste. Missing ratings are predicted by reconstructing from the low-rank approximation. Netflix Prize winner used matrix factorization as a core technique.

kappa(A) = sigma_max / sigma_min. If kappa = 10^6, a 0.001% change in b can cause up to a 0.001% * 10^6 = 1000% change in the solution x. You lose approximately log10(kappa) digits of accuracy. Machine epsilon is about 10^(-16), so kappa > 10^8 means you lose more than half your significant digits.

Rank: number of nonzero singular values. Column space: columns of U for nonzero sigma. Null space: columns of V for zero sigma. Pseudoinverse: invert nonzero sigma. Least squares: use pseudoinverse. Best approximation: truncate small sigma. Condition number: ratio of extreme sigma. The SVD is a complete structural analysis of any matrix.

questions.question: Describe geometrically what the matrix [[0, -1], [1, 0]] does to vectors in R^2. Identify the transformation type and the specific parameter (angle or axis).
questions.answer: This matrix rotates vectors 90 degrees counterclockwise about the origin. Verification by applying to basis vectors: e_1 = [1, 0] maps to [0*1 + (-1)*0, 1*1 + 0*0] = [0, 1]. The positive x-axis maps to the positive y-axis. e_2 = [0, 1] maps to [0*0 + (-1)*1, 1*0 + 0*1] = [-1, 0]. The positive y-axis maps to the negative x-axis. Both mappings are consistent with a 90-degree counterclockwise rotation. The general rotation matrix for angle theta is R(theta) = [[cos(theta), -sin(theta)], [sin(theta), cos(theta)]]. Setting theta = pi/2: cos(pi/2) = 0, sin(pi/2) = 1, giving [[0, -1], [1, 0]]. This confirms the transformation is rotation by pi/2 radians (90 degrees) counterclockwise. Additional properties: det = 0*0 - (-1)*1 = 1, confirming the transformation preserves area and orientation. Eigenvalues are i and -i (complex), confirming pure rotation with no stretching.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the transformation as rotation
Correct angle (90 degrees counterclockwise) with verification
Connection to general rotation formula or basis vector verification

questions.question: Compute det(A) for A = [[1, 2, 3], [0, 4, 5], [0, 0, 6]] and det(B) for B = [[2, 1, 3], [4, 2, 7], [6, 3, 5]]. For A, use the triangular property. For B, use row reduction. Then compute det(AB) and explain your method.
questions.answer: det(A): A is upper triangular, so det(A) = product of diagonal entries = 1 * 4 * 6 = 24. det(B) by row reduction: B = [[2, 1, 3], [4, 2, 7], [6, 3, 5]]. R2 = R2 - 2*R1: [[2, 1, 3], [0, 0, 1], [6, 3, 5]]. R3 = R3 - 3*R1: [[2, 1, 3], [0, 0, 1], [0, 0, -4]]. R3 = R3 + 4*R2: [[2, 1, 3], [0, 0, 1], [0, 0, 0]]. The matrix has a row of zeros, so det(B) = 0. (Alternatively, note that R3 of B = R1 + R2: [6, 3, 5] = [2, 1, 3] + [4, 2, 7] would give [6, 3, 10] != [6, 3, 5], so let me recheck. Actually: R2 - 2*R1 = [4-4, 2-2, 7-6] = [0, 0, 1]. R3 - 3*R1 = [6-6, 3-3, 5-9] = [0, 0, -4]. R3 + 4*R2 = [0, 0, -4+4] = [0, 0, 0]. So the third row reduces to zero, confirming det(B) = 0.) det(AB) = det(A) * det(B) = 24 * 0 = 0. The product property det(AB) = det(A) * det(B) applies to any two square matrices of the same size. Since det(B) = 0, B is singular, and therefore AB is also singular with determinant 0.

rubric.key: Correct det(A) using triangular property
Correct row reduction for det(B) with sign tracking
Correct det(AB) using the product property with explanation

questions.question: Explain why computing A^(-1)b to solve Ax = b is less efficient than using LU decomposition. Compare the operation counts for solving a single system and for solving 100 systems with the same A but different b vectors.
questions.answer: Computing A^(-1): Row reducing [A | I] to [I | A^(-1)] requires approximately 2n^3 operations (twice the cost of standard Gaussian elimination because the right-hand side is an n x n matrix). Then multiplying A^(-1) * b requires n^2 operations. Total for one system: 2n^3 + n^2. For 100 systems: 2n^3 + 100*n^2 (compute inverse once, multiply 100 times). LU decomposition: Computing A = LU requires approximately (2/3)n^3 operations. Solving Ly = b (forward substitution) requires approximately n^2/2 operations. Solving Ux = y (back substitution) requires approximately n^2/2 operations. Total for one system: (2/3)n^3 + n^2. For 100 systems: (2/3)n^3 + 100*n^2 (factor once, solve 100 times). Comparison for a single system: LU uses (2/3)n^3 operations while inverse uses 2n^3, making LU about 3 times faster. For 100 systems: LU uses (2/3)n^3 + 100n^2 while inverse uses 2n^3 + 100n^2. The 100n^2 term is the same, but LU saves (4/3)n^3 in the factorization step, still about 3 times faster for the expensive part. Additionally, computing A^(-1) introduces more numerical error because it involves more floating-point operations. LU decomposition is also more numerically stable, especially with partial pivoting.

rubric.key: Correct operation counts for inverse computation and LU factorization
Correct comparison for single system and multiple systems
Mention of numerical stability advantage of LU

questions.question: Prove that the set of all 2x2 symmetric matrices forms a subspace of the vector space of all 2x2 matrices. What is the dimension of this subspace? Give a basis.
questions.answer: Let S = {A in R^(2x2) : A = A^T} be the set of 2x2 symmetric matrices. Subspace proof (three conditions): (1) Zero vector: The 2x2 zero matrix satisfies 0^T = 0, so the zero matrix is in S. The set is nonempty. (2) Closure under addition: Let A, B be in S, so A^T = A and B^T = B. Then (A + B)^T = A^T + B^T = A + B. So A + B is symmetric, meaning A + B is in S. (3) Closure under scalar multiplication: Let A be in S and c be a scalar. Then (cA)^T = c * A^T = c * A = cA. So cA is symmetric, meaning cA is in S. Since all three conditions are satisfied, S is a subspace of R^(2x2). Dimension: A 2x2 symmetric matrix has the form [[a, b], [b, c]] with three independent parameters a, b, c. Therefore dim(S) = 3. Basis: E_1 = [[1, 0], [0, 0]], E_2 = [[0, 1], [1, 0]], E_3 = [[0, 0], [0, 1]]. These are symmetric, linearly independent (no nontrivial linear combination equals zero), and span S (any [[a, b], [b, c]] = a*E_1 + b*E_2 + c*E_3).

rubric.key: Complete subspace proof with all three conditions verified
Correct dimension with justification
Valid basis with verification of independence and spanning

questions.question: A data matrix X has 1000 rows (observations) and 50 columns (features). After computing the RREF, you find 8 pivot columns. Interpret this finding in terms of the data: what does the rank tell you, and what are the practical implications for dimensionality reduction?
questions.answer: The rank of X is 8, meaning only 8 of the 50 features are linearly independent. The remaining 42 features are exact linear combinations of these 8. This means: (1) Information content: the data lives in an 8-dimensional subspace of R^50. All 1000 data points can be exactly represented using only 8 coordinates (in the right basis), despite having 50 measured features. (2) Redundancy: 42 features provide zero additional information beyond what the 8 independent features contain. This could indicate highly correlated measurements, derived features (e.g., total = quantity * price), or systematic relationships in the data. (3) Dimensionality reduction: PCA or SVD can project the data from 50 dimensions to 8 dimensions with zero information loss. The top 8 principal components capture 100% of the variance. This dramatically reduces storage (1000 x 8 instead of 1000 x 50), computation time for downstream algorithms, and visualization complexity. (4) Practical implications: if training a machine learning model, using all 50 features wastes computation and may cause multicollinearity issues (since 42 columns are linearly dependent on the other 8, the feature matrix is rank-deficient and ordinary least squares fails). Reduce to the 8 independent features first. (5) Null space: the null space of X has dimension 50 - 8 = 42, meaning there are 42 linearly independent directions in feature space that produce zero change in the data representation.

rubric.key: Correct interpretation of rank as number of independent features
Connection to PCA and dimensionality reduction with zero information loss
Practical implications for modeling (multicollinearity, computation)
